---
title: "Assignment 1 - Marketing campaign report"
editor: visual
author: Duc Hoang
date: today
format: 
    html:
        toc: true
        toc-title: "Table of Contents"
        theme: litera
    pdf:
        documentclass: article
        fontsize: 11pt
        geometry:                 
        - top=25mm
        - left=25mm
        - right=25mm
        - bottom=25mm
        number-sections: true
execute:
    echo: false
    warning: false
    message: false
---

```{r}

library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)
library(here)
library(dplyr)
```

## 1. Introduction
For the last quarter, Marketing department had lauched a campaign of personalised marketing, calculate the  marketing spent on each customers.

This report will contrast customer acquisition metrics, such as store visits and transaction volumes directly against their associated marketing expenditures to evaluate whether more marketing cost per customer bring about more benefits. It examines a comprehensive cross-section of our user base, evaluating cohorts ranging from new acquisitions to tenured accounts.

The scope of these observations includes market segmentation across individual consumers to enterprise clients.

## 2. Research objectives
This report analyzes the collected dataset to evaluate the key drivers of financial performance and customer value. 

- First, the investigation will focus on answering if there were **a correlation exists between <span style="color:red">Marketing expense</span> and <span style="color:red">Gross revenue</span>**, in order to assess the financial efficiency of our promotional campaigns. 
- Afterwards, **<span style="color:red">Customer segments</span>** will be examined distinctively to **identify differences in <span style="color:red">Revenue</span> across these segments**, identifying the campaign's most profitable target audiences.

## 3. Dataset overview 
### Data cleaning procedures
The dataset is clearly not clean enough to build an analysis pipeline on. Here are the cleaning procedures specifically applied to this dataset:

- **Step 1: Set up a relative path to the dataset**: 
In order for the report to function well across other devices, the path for the dataset have to be set to relative pathway.
```{r}
#| include: false

data <- read_csv(file = here::here("data", "36558966_Duc Hoang.csv"))
```

- **Step 2: Format date field**: 
For datasets involving `date/time` fields, the ultimate cleaning process is to set the value of these colunms into appropriate format. We are looking for `date` or `date/time` format depending on each dataset. In this case, the dataset originally contained inconsistent date formats within the `signup_date` field. To ensure accurate reporting and time-series analysis, all values have been standardized to a uniform *"YYYY-MM-DD format"*.
```{r}
#| include: false

data_date_clean <- data |> 
    mutate(
        clean_date = parse_date_time(signup_date, orders = c("ymd", "dmy", "mdy")),
        signup_date = as.Date(clean_date)
    )
print(data_date_clean, n = 150)
```

- **Step 3: Clean NA records**
```{r}
#| include: false

na_purchase_data <- filter(data_date_clean, is.na(purchase_amount))
print(na_purchase_data)

# For records that have the notes == returned, we will add the purchase amt = 0. For the rest, we grop them observations
```

Take a look into the dataset, there are **`r nrow(na_purchase_data)` records** missing a value in the `purchase_amount` field. Within this subset, two records include the `notes = returned` (@fig-na_purchase). Further investigation indicates that the `purchase_amount` column tracks gross sales rather than net sales; this is evidenced by numerous other `returned` records that still retain their original, positive purchase values (@fig-return_data). Consequently, we cannot accurately impute a zero value for these two entries. Because the remaining null records also lack sufficient context for accurate imputation, all missing values in this field will be left as-is to preserve data integrity.

```{r}
#| label: fig-na_purchase
#| fig-cap: "Records missing purchase amount value"
#| fig-cap-location: bottom
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("images", "NA_purchase_data.png"))
```
```{r}
#| label: fig-return_data
#| fig-cap: "Customers have had their purchases returned"
#| fig-cap-location: bottom 
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("images", "return_data.png"))
```

Also, to maintain consistency across the dataset, manual text entries of `N/A` within the notes column were standardized to the system's official missing value format `NA` (@fig-na-notes).

```{r}
#| label: fig-na-notes
#| fig-cap: "Records having notes value of N/A"
#| fig-cap-location: bottom
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("images", "na_notes.png"))
```

```{r}
#| include: false

data_date_clean$notes[data_date_clean$notes == "N/A"] <- NA
clean_data <- data_date_clean
```

### Overview
This quarterly dataset captures individual customer engagement and financial transactions to evaluate marketing efficiency and customer value. Each row represents one unique customer, detailing metrics relating to their behaviour on the retail website (visits, purchase volume) alongside the specific marketing initiatives they received (ads, coupons, email management fee, and after-sevice). To quantify revenue generation, the data measures gross sales via the `purchase_amount` field, while supplementary `notes` capture specific transactional behaviors such as product returns. Overall, these records provide the necessary foundation for assessing marketing return on investment (ROI) across various customer segments.

### Detailed description
The dataset 

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: tbl-summarise-table
#| tbl-cap: "Total retail metrics by customer's segment"
#| tbl-colwidths: [60,40]
sum_by_segments <- clean_data |> 
    group_by(segment) |> 
    summarise(
        marketing_expense = sum(marketing_spend),
        total_visits = sum(visits),
        gross_revenue = sum(purchase_amount, na.rm = TRUE)
    )
knitr::kable(sum_by_segments, digit = 2)
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-summarise-plot
#| fig-cap: "Pie chart for customer in each segment"
#| fig-cap-location: bottom

mean_by_segments <- clean_data |> 
    group_by(segment) |> 
    summarise(customer_count = n())

ggplot(mean_by_segments, aes(x = "", y = customer_count, fill = segment)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 1) +
  coord_polar("y", start = 0) +
  theme_void() + 
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(customer_count / sum(customer_count), accuracy = 1)), 
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), 
            color = "white",
            size = 5)

```

## 4. Analysis result
